title: Easter Sunday
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## Easter Sunday, April 17, 2022
## Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

Easter Sunday, the principal feast of the Christian year celebrating the Resurrection of Jesus Christ. The RCL readings were Isaiah 65:17-25, Psalm 118:1-2, 14-24, 1 Corinthians 15:19-26, and John 20:19-31, focusing on resurrection themes and the appearance of the risen Christ to the disciples. This falls at the end of the Easter Season proper, beginning the fifty days of Eastertide that would continue through Pentecost on June 5, 2022.

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of its 80th General Convention (held July 2021 in Baltimore), where significant resolutions on LGBTQ+ inclusion and racial justice had been passed but remained controversial in some dioceses. The Diocese of Colorado was relatively stable during this period, though the national church continued internal discussions around sexuality and marriage doctrine. No major diocesan leadership changes or crises were occurring in spring 2022.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in spring weather transition with temperatures in the 50s-60s Fahrenheit the week before Easter. No major natural disasters or weather emergencies affected the area. Local political focus was on the May 2022 Colorado primary elections (scheduled for June 28), with gubernatorial and congressional races beginning to intensify, though early April saw relatively modest campaign activity.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The war in Ukraine dominated headlines, now six weeks into Russia's invasion with devastating civilian casualties in towns like Bucha where mass graves were being discovered that week (April 10-15). Domestic inflation remained a critical issue, with the US inflation rate at 8.5% year-over-year in March 2022, the highest in 40 years. President Biden was navigating inflation concerns while supporting Ukraine aid packages; Congress was working on a $40 billion supplemental Ukraine assistance bill passed in late April.

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Ukraine war was the dominant international story, with Russian forces withdrawing from areas around Kyiv after failing to capture the capital, leaving evidence of widespread atrocities that shocked the world. China was implementing strict zero-COVID lockdowns in Shanghai, beginning April 4, affecting 26+ million residents and disrupting global supply chains. North Korea was conducting missile tests in response to US-South Korean joint exercises.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The American mood was anxious and polarized: the Ukraine war had temporarily unified some political divisions but economic anxiety from inflation was rising. The country was entering what felt like a "return to normal" post-Omicron, with mask mandates ending and cultural life resuming, though collective trauma from two years of pandemic persisted. The 2022 Academy Awards (March 27) had been overshadowed by Will Smith slapping Chris Rock on stage, dominating cultural conversation for weeks and still being actively discussed in mid-April. Sports culture was focused on March Madness aftermath and opening baseball season.
